text: The surgeon uses multiple ports for laparoscopic dissection, including left and right upper and lower quadrant ports, to facilitate mobilization of the colon. The harmonic scalpel is used to dissect around the hepatic flexure, with the right ureter visible and cleared of surrounding tissue. The ENSEAL instrument is employed to divide the gastrocolic omentum, and the transverse mesocolon is taken with an endoscopic linear cutter. The spleen and splenic flexure are visible as the surgeon mobilizes the left colon using the harmonic scalpel, ensuring the left ureter is exposed. The procedure involves intracorporeal dissection with energy sources, focusing on the colon wall rather than proximal vessels, and concludes with mobilization of the entire abdominal colon.

The surgeon encounters issues with the stapler and clip, opting to use an endo-loop for hemostasis. The dissection proceeds into the pelvis and pre-sacral space, with the harmonic scalpel used to navigate the avascular plane in front of the sacrum and behind the mesorectum. The surgeon carefully reflects a nerve laterally and manages the large uterus with a clamp to maintain visualization. The rectum is transected, and the specimen is extracted through the perineum, facilitating the creation of a tension-free ileostomy. The operation concludes with the maturation of the brook ileostomy.
